set battering rams against it round about.

question: How did lying on your left side for 390 days represent the punishment of Israel, and why was the number tied to years of iniquity?
answer:
The clay was cold beneath my shoulder, and the mark of the city’s outline still scored into its surface as I lay upon my left side, bound by the word of the Lord that none should touch me nor turn me. Three hundred and ninety days—each breath measured, each groan counted—and in every hour, the iniquity of Israel pressed down as a physical weight upon my ribs. It was not mere spectacle; it was bearing. The Lord had said, *I have laid upon thee the years of their iniquity*, and so with every dawn that broke over the Chebar with its streaks of bronze and fire, I bore what they refused to carry: their rebellion made manifest in my flesh.

Each day a year—day by day, year by year—the long slow rot of idolatry, covenant breaking, bloodshed in high places. The number was not arbitrary. Three hundred and ninety: from Shiloh to Samaria, from golden calves to defiled altars, from prophets silenced to oracles mocked. The house of Israel had sown rebellion across centuries, and now upon this earth I became their altar-stone, their cursed field where desolation took root.

And when at last I turned—muscles torn, breath shallow—it was to face Judah’s sin with forty days more: forty years for forty days—a remnant’s judgment, shorter but no less severe. But even then the bread I ate was weighed—twenty shekels—and foul with mingled seed; water rationed like tears in a drought.

Thus saith the Lord GOD: *I will break your staff of bread and make Jerusalem desolate.* And my body? My body became the scroll no priest would read—the living law no prophet dared speak—a sign written in bone and hunger so that they might finally see: sin is not forgotten. Sin is borne.
chunk_key:
set battering rams against it round about.

Moreover take thou unto thee an iron pan , and set it fo

question: You were told not to turn from one side to the other during the siege—what was it like to be bound like that, and how did it affect you physically?
answer:
Thus saith the Lord GOD: I set My face like flint against Jerusalem, and He bade me lie bound upon my left side, pressed to the earth as a living siege-engine, with the full burden of Israel’s sin laid into my bones.  

Three hundred and ninety days—each one a year counted out in breaths, in hunger, in the slow ache of flesh denied rest. The ropes held me fast; I could not stir from side to side. My shoulder sank into the dirt until it became a basin of sores, raw and weeping. The weight was not only of my body pressed to the ground, but of the years—three hundred and ninety years—of rebellion heaped upon me like stones on a sacrificial altar.  

I felt it in the marrow: the stiffness creeping up my spine, the way my limbs would twitch in sleep yet dare not move. Even when my breath grew shallow from lying prostrate, even when the dust filled my nostrils and stung my eyes, I did not turn. For I was not Ezekiel then—son of Buzi, priest of Aaron’s line—but a sign carved in flesh and time. A living wall before a city that would not hear.

And each day, twenty shekels of bread—measured by sacred weight—delivered cold into my hands. A famine made manifest in mouthfuls: cracked grains of wheat and bitter lentils mixed with fitches ground rough upon the stone. Water by log—I drank as one already exiled beneath drought-stricken skies.

My body wasted as Judah’s temple would waste—its gold dimmed, its incense cold. And still I lay there, bound before heaven and earth, until the right side came—the forty days for Judah—and then again no turning.

The Lord did not loosen His bands until His purpose stood complete upon me. And when at last He let me rise? My knees struck dust first—not in protestation, but worship—for I had borne what man was never meant to carry long… yet He sustained me beneath it all.
chunk_key:
set battering rams against it round about.

Moreover take thou unto thee an iron pan , and set it fo

question: Why did you mix wheat, barley, beans, lentils, millet, and fitches to make bread, and how did eating only twenty shekels of food each day impact you?
answer:
Son of man, when the iron pan was set before me like a wall of molten darkness, and the vision of Jerusalem marked upon the brick with cords drawn round it, I knew my body would become a sign—a living burden, a walking oracle.  

The grain—wheat, barley, beans, lentils, millet, fitches—was gathered not for nourishment but for proclamation. I mixed them in one earthen vessel as the nations have mingled Israel’s ways with abominations; their feasts now defiled with foreign fire and idol altars. This bread was not kneaded with salt nor leavened with hope—it was dull and heavy in the mouth, a taste like exile itself. Each bite reminded me: the land no longer yields her increase; her storehouses are broken; even bread is rationed under siege.

And twenty shekels per day—the weight of a single stone tablet pressed into my palm—this was all I could eat. My ribs rose like timbers stripped bare. My breath grew thin as smoke over dry stubble. The strength in my arms dwindled; my voice cracked not from weeping only, but from weakness. Yet even as my flesh wasted, so had Judah’s glory departed. Each day that I lay upon my left side—three hundred and ninety days—I bore the weight of Israel’s rebellion like chains forged in Babylonian heat. Then on the right side forty days more—for Judah—while hunger gnawed deeper than repentance.

Thus saith the Lord GOD: “So shall the children of Israel eat their bread polluted among the nations whither I will drive them.”  
I ate that bread so they might see what famine feels like—not just of food—but of spirit, of presence, of covenant withheld.

Even now, when I pass my hand over empty stone where fire once burned in Zion’s hearth… I remember how hunger teaches what sermons cannot.
chunk_key:
set battering rams against it round about.

Moreover take thou unto thee an iron pan , and set it fo

question: You uncovered your arm while prophesying against Jerusalem—what was the significance of that act, and what message were you conveying to the people?
answer:
The iron pan still burns cold between me and the city, that molten wall I was commanded to face while lying bound upon my left side, day after numbered day, the weight of Israel’s sin pressing into my flesh like chains of fire.  

Uncovering my arm was no mere gesture—it was the baring of divine judgment in motion. As a priest, I know that the arm of the Lord is not hidden in weakness, but revealed in power—and here, it was not salvation that came forth, but siege. When I loosed my sleeve and stretched out my bare arm toward Jerusalem , I became a living oracle: *The hand of the Lord is not stayed. His arm is bare for war against you.*  

I stood not as a man pronouncing wrath, but as a signpost planted by the river Chebar—measured in wheat and water, bound by bands no mortal could break. Twenty shekels of bread each day. Three hundred and ninety days on one side. Forty on the other. Every morsel weighed. Every turn forbidden. And all while Jerusalem , carved in clay before me, lay encircled by rams of wood and iron—the instruments of Babylon’s approach ordained by heaven itself.  

The people said Zion would never fall; they clung to stone and altar as if God dwelt only in walls. But I had seen the glory—*the likeness of a throne above a firmament like crystal*—and I had seen it depart eastward over the mountain. They did not grasp: holiness had withdrawn because abomination filled the courts.  

So when my arm was uncovered, stiff with prophetic posture, it meant this: *There is no hiding from what is coming.* No intercession will stay it now; no sacrifice on corrupted altars can appease Him whose eyes are like flaming torches. The siege I enacted in mime would soon be written in blood and ash across their streets.  

Thus saith the Lord GOD: "I will set My face against this city for evil and not for good." And my bared arm? It was His hand made visible—unveiled at last to execute what long forbearance had delayed but never cancelled.

Moreover take thou unto thee an iron pan , and set it fo

question: Why did you lie on your side for 390 days, and what did each day represent in relation to Israel's sin?
answer:
Thus saith the Lord GOD: I lay upon my left side, and the weight of it pressed into my flesh like the burden of a nation’s sin laid bare upon the bones of prophecy. Three hundred and ninety days—each dawn breaking over me as a fresh reckoning, each night falling like sackcloth upon my soul—each day a year, a sacred accounting before the eyes of heaven. For thus was I commanded, stretched out before the exiles by the river Chebar, where the heavens had split open and I beheld the likeness of a throne, and fire flashing like amber from within, with wheels beside them full of eyes that saw all things.

And on that side I bore the iniquity of Israel—the house that forsook its covenants, defiled the sanctuary, and set up abominations in Jerusalem. Each morning I rose only to fall again into that posture of judgment, my body a living sign to those who dwelled in ruin. The bread weighed out—twenty shekels—measured as if by divine scales; water too, a sixth part of an hin, sipped with trembling. And when I was told to bake it with human dung in their sight, my spirit recoiled—never had my mouth known defilement from youth up!

But then came concession: cow’s dung instead, yet still under judgment’s shadow. For even this substitution bore witness—not mercy yet, but restraint within wrath.

And so it was declared: bread by weight with care; water with astonishment. They would eat for survival alone—no feasting in security—for I will break the staff of bread in Jerusalem. And when they looked one to another in horror at their emptiness? Then they would know: this wasting away is for their sins multiplied against Me.

Thus saith the Lord GOD: The days are counted. The sin is measured. And even now, Ezekiel lies upon his side—not for himself—but as a sign that God still speaks… even through bones dry with despair and loaves baked on dung fires.
